choices.D: '' and True — string defaults apply, but circularity is inferred true

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation: In a dataclass, fields with default values are optional during construction. sequence has no default and is required; ext5 and is_circular use their declared defaults of '' and False. Option B confuses omitted fields with None. Option C would apply only if those fields had no defaults. Option D invents biological logic that the class does not encode.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Understanding how frozen dataclasses enforce immutability and why that matters for data integrity
schemas_identity_and_serialization.question:
A strain genotype is modeled as a frozen dataclass:

<python>
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Strain:
    name: str
    genotype: tuple

ecoli = Strain(name='DH10B', genotype=('lacZ-', 'recA1'))
</python>

Later, a function tries to record a new mutation:

<python>
ecoli.genotype = ('lacZ-', 'recA1', 'endA1')
</python>

What happens?

choices.A: The assignment succeeds, and genotype now includes the new endA1 entry
choices.B: A TypeError is raised because tuples cannot be modified in place here
choices.C: The assignment returns a new Strain object while leaving ecoli unchanged
choices.D: FrozenInstanceError is raised because frozen dataclasses block reassignment

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation: <python>frozen=True</python> makes dataclass attributes read-only after construction. Reassigning <python>ecoli.genotype</python> raises <python>FrozenInstanceError</python> (a subclass of AttributeError). The failure is due to dataclass immutability, not tuple immutability.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Enforcing invariants on object fields to prevent construction of invalid biological objects

choices.A: Polynucleotide(sequence='AUGCCC') — one character is outside A/T/C/G
choices.B: Polynucleotide(sequence='ATGCCC') — all characters are valid DNA bases
choices.C: Polynucleotide(sequence='ATCG') — all characters match the allowed set
choices.D: Polynucleotide(sequence='GGGGGGGG') — repeated G bases are still allowed

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation:
The regex r'[ATCG]+' allows only DNA bases A, T, C, and G, and requires at least one character. 
AUGCCC
contains U, so it fails validation and raises ValueError. The other sequences contain only valid DNA bases and pass.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Identifying the minimal set of fields needed to uniquely identify and describe a shared resource
schemas_identity_and_serialization.question:
In the C9 system, all Sharables (models, data, functions, views) carry a
common set of identity fields: id, type, name, description, and keywords.

A student proposes dropping the id field and using name alone as the
unique identifier, arguing that names are human-readable. Why is a
separate id field necessary?

choices.A: Id stores sequence content directly, while names cannot store sequences
choices.B: Python requires explicit ids on objects to support memory management
choices.C: Name is always optional here, so it can never identify any sharable
choices.D: Names can change or collide, but stable ids preserve durable references

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation: Names are human-friendly, but they are not guaranteed to be unique or stable. A separate id provides a persistent reference that still works if a name changes or if multiple objects share similar names. This is the same idea as accession numbers in sequence databases.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Using schema definitions to validate data types and prevent silent errors in serialized data
schemas_identity_and_serialization.question:
A JSON schema for a Polynucleotide requires: 
{
  "sequence": {"type": "string"},
  "is_circular": {"type": "boolean"}
}
A researcher exports their construct as JSON, but their script writes is_circular as the string "true" instead of the boolean true: 
{"sequence": "ATGAAATAA", "is_circular": "true"}
What should happen when this JSON is validated against the schema?

choices.A: Validation passes because JSON treats string and boolean forms alike
choices.B: Validation passes because schema validation ignores mismatched field types
choices.C: Validation fails because the schema requires a boolean, not a string
choices.D: Validation fails because the quoted word true is invalid JSON syntax

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation:
JSON distinguishes between strings and booleans. The schema explicitly requires a boolean for is_circular, so the string 
"true"
fails validation. This prevents subtle downstream bugs, such as treating the string 
"false"
as truthy in Python.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Parsing a shorthand notation that uses names to reference objects and resolving them in order
schemas_identity_and_serialization.question:
A ConstructionFile uses CF shorthand where each line defines a new DNA by name, and subsequent lines can reference previously defined DNAs: 
PCR p1 p2 pUC19   -> frag1
PCR p3 p4 genomic -> frag2
Gibson frag1 frag2 -> product
The Gibson step references frag1 and frag2 by name. What happens if the lines are reordered so the Gibson step appears before the PCR steps?

choices.A: It still works because names are resolved after the full file is parsed
choices.B: It fails because frag1 and frag2 are undefined at that parse position
choices.C: It works if frag1 and frag2 already exist in a separate global registry
choices.D: It runs, but the fragment order is swapped during later name resolution

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation:
CF shorthand is parsed top-to-bottom. A name becomes available only after the line that defines it has been parsed. If the Gibson line appears first, 
frag1
and 
frag2
cannot be resolved at that point.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Ensuring that serialization and deserialization preserve all semantically meaningful distinctions
schemas_identity_and_serialization.question:
Two Polynucleotide objects differ only in circularity:

<python>
linear = Polynucleotide(sequence='ATGAAATAA', is_circular=False)
circular = Polynucleotide(sequence='ATGAAATAA', is_circular=True)
</python>

A student writes a serializer that saves only the sequence to a FASTA file
and a deserializer that reconstructs the object with default values:

<python>
def serialize(poly):
    return f'>construct\n{poly.sequence}'

def deserialize(fasta_text):
    seq = fasta_text.split('\n')[1]
    return Polynucleotide(sequence=seq)
</python>

What happens when the circular plasmid is round-tripped through
serialize then deserialize?

choices.A: It reconstructs as circular because FASTA preserves circularity metadata
choices.B: It changes the sequence itself because deserialization reorders bases
choices.C: It reconstructs as linear because circularity was not serialized at all
choices.D: It raises ValueError because FASTA cannot represent DNA sequences safely

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation: The serializer writes only the sequence and discards the <python>is_circular</python> field. On deserialization, the object is rebuilt using defaults, so <python>is_circular</python> becomes False. This is a loss of semantics during round-trip serialization.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Handling the case where a referenced identifier does not exist in the registry
schemas_identity_and_serialization.question:
A ConstructionFile references a plasmid by name: 
PCR oJC101 oJC102 pSB1C3 -> pcr_product
The parser looks up 
pSB1C3
in a local registry dict: template = registry['pSB1C3'] But the researcher forgot to add pSB1C3 to the registry before running the parser. What happens, and what is the best handling behavior?

choices.A: It returns None, and parsing continues with a missing template object
choices.B: It fetches pSB1C3 automatically from an external sequence repository
choices.C: It raises KeyError, and parser code should report the missing DNA name
choices.D: It creates a blank placeholder Polynucleotide and continues parsing

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation: Accessing a missing dict key raises KeyError. The parser should catch that and report a clear message identifying the missing name (and ideally the source line), rather than continuing with invalid placeholder data.

schemas_identity_and_serialization.topic: Understanding how optional fields modify the interpretation of a core required field
schemas_identity_and_serialization.question:
A Polynucleotide has a required sequence field and optional ext5 and ext3
fields representing single-stranded overhangs:

<python>
frag = Polynucleotide(
    sequence='ATGAAAGGGCCC',
    ext5='AATT',
    ext3='',
    is_double_stranded=True
)
</python>

In the physical DNA this represents, what is the structure at the 5' end
versus the 3' end?

choices.A: 5' has a 4-nt overhang, while 3' is blunt with no extension present
choices.B: Both ends are blunt because ext5/ext3 are labels, not structural fields
choices.C: 5' is blunt, while 3' has a 4-nt overhang from the given extension
choices.D: Both ends have 4-nt overhangs because ext3 mirrors ext5 by default

schemas_identity_and_serialization.explanation: <python>ext5='AATT'</python> indicates a 4-nt single-stranded extension on the 5' end. <python>ext3=''</python> means no 3' extension, so the 3' end is blunt. The extension fields change the physical interpretation of the same core double-stranded sequence.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Recognizing how mutating shared state during run breaks repeatability and violates a clean computation contract

choices.A: The method should return the correction factor too, so callers can verify the temperature adjustment each time
choices.B: The temperature logic should be in __init__, so setup and validation happen before any calculation runs
choices.C: The method should avoid floats here, because repeated decimal math makes scientific results drift over time
choices.D: The method mutates shared state while computing, so later outputs depend on prior calls, not just inputs

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation:
The flaw is that <python>run()</python> changes <python>self.correction_factor</python> while computing the result. Because that state persists on the object, later calls can produce different outputs for the same inputs depending on what was run earlier.

For example, a call with temperature 30 increases the correction factor. A later call at 20C applies no new correction, but still uses the previously modified factor. Another call at 30C adds correction again on top of the already changed value. This breaks repeatability and makes the method behave like a stateful accumulator rather than a clean computation from explicit inputs.

The core issue is hidden state mutation during <python>run()</python>, not the use of classes, methods, or floating-point numbers.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Understanding that a function signature and docstring define a contract between the implementer and every caller
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
A codon optimization function is documented as:

<python>
def optimize_codons(cds: str, organism: str) -> str:
    '''Accepts a DNA coding sequence and a host organism name.
    Returns a codon-optimized DNA sequence of the same length.
    Raises ValueError if cds length is not a multiple of 3.'''
    ...
</python>

A colleague calls the function and writes these checks:

<python>
result = optimize_codons('ATGAAAGGG', 'ecoli')
assert len(result) == len('ATGAAAGGG')
assert result.startswith('ATG')
</python>

Why is the length assertion justified but the startswith assertion risky?

choices.A: The contract guarantees same-length output but does not promise the first codon stays unchanged
choices.B: The contract guarantees both checks because any codon optimizer must preserve an initial ATG codon
choices.C: The contract guarantees neither check because a return type of str permits any possible string
choices.D: The contract guarantees startswith('ATG') because biological convention always requires a start codon

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation:
The docstring explicitly guarantees that the returned sequence has the same length as the input, so the length assertion is contract-safe. The contract says nothing about preserving any specific codon, including the start codon. A real implementation would usually preserve 
ATG
, but callers should not rely on behavior the API does not explicitly promise. Option B assumes a biological fact that is outside the stated contract. Option C ignores the explicit same-length guarantee. Option D confuses biological convention with an API guarantee.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Recognizing why separating pure computation from I/O makes functions reusable and testable
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
Two approaches to computing the melting temperature of a primer:

<python>
# Version A
def get_tm_from_user():
    seq = input('Enter primer sequence: ')
    tm = 2 * sum(1 for b in seq if b in 'AT') + 4 * sum(1 for b in seq if b in 'GC')
    print(f'Tm = {tm}')

# Version B
def compute_tm(seq: str) -> float:
    return 2 * sum(1 for b in seq if b in 'AT') + 4 * sum(1 for b in seq if b in 'GC')
</python>

A design pipeline needs to compute Tm for 500 primers stored in a list.
Which version can it use directly?

choices.A: Version A, because a loop can call it repeatedly and read each primer through interactive input
choices.B: Version B, because it accepts a sequence argument and returns a value for any data source
choices.C: Both versions, because printing a value and returning a value are interchangeable in pipelines
choices.D: Neither version, because the Wallace-rule formula is too approximate for practical automation

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: Version A mixes computation with I/O: it reads from the keyboard and prints to the console. That makes it hard to use inside a pipeline that already has the sequences in memory and needs the numeric result back. Version B cleanly separates computation from I/O by taking an input value and returning an output value. This makes it reusable in loops, tests, web apps, notebooks, and other functions. Option A fails because <python>input()</python> blocks for interactive entry. Option C confuses a side effect (<python>print</python>) with a returned value. Option D critiques the model, but the question is about API design and reusability.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Identifying the guarantees a pure function provides and why they matter for reproducible pipelines
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
A gene design pipeline calls three functions in sequence:

<python>
def codon_optimize(cds, codon_table):
    '''Pure function: returns optimized CDS.'''
    ...

def log_to_database(cds, timestamp):
    '''Writes the CDS and timestamp to a remote database.'''
    ...

def add_flanking_sequences(cds, vector):
    '''Pure function: returns CDS with appropriate flanks.'''
    ...
</python>

The pipeline runs once and produces a correct result. The server hosting
the database later goes offline. What happens when the pipeline is rerun
with identical inputs?

choices.A: All three functions fail because the database outage propagates through the entire pipeline run
choices.B: The pure functions return the same outputs, while log_to_database fails due to the outage
choices.C: The pure functions return different outputs because cached database state changed after run one
choices.D: All three functions succeed because Python automatically retries network calls until they work

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: Pure functions depend only on their inputs and have no side effects, so they are unaffected by external failures like a database outage. With identical inputs, <python>codon_optimize</python> and <python>add_flanking_sequences</python> should return the same outputs as before. <python>log_to_database</python> performs network I/O and fails when the database is unavailable. This is why pipelines are typically designed with a pure computational core and I/O at the edges. Option A incorrectly treats pure functions as externally dependent. Option C invents a hidden dependency not stated in the code. Option D invents automatic retry behavior.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Distinguishing the roles of host, client, and server in a layered protocol architecture
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
In the Model Context Protocol (MCP), three roles cooperate: - Server: exposes capabilities (Resources, Tools, Prompts) - Client: maintains a 1:1 connection with a server - Host: the user-facing application that coordinates clients A bioinformatics lab runs an MCP server that exposes a codon optimization Tool. A researcher uses an LLM-powered assistant (the host) connected to that server. The LLM proposes calling the codon optimization tool for the sequence 
ATGAAACCC
. Who decides whether the tool is actually invoked?

choices.A: The server decides because it exposes the tool and therefore controls whether each call runs
choices.B: The LLM decides because proposing the tool call in its response is the same as execution
choices.C: The host decides because it mediates requests and applies approval policy before execution
choices.D: The client decides because it automatically executes any tool request generated by the LLM

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: In MCP, the LLM proposes actions, but the host is responsible for applying approval policies before any tool call is executed. The host mediates between the user, the model, and the client connection. Option A confuses exposing a tool with deciding whether to execute a particular invocation. Option B treats a proposal as execution. Option D skips the host approval layer that is central to controlled tool use.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Distinguishing read-only data access from actions that produce side effects
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
An MCP server for a genetic design platform exposes the following:

Resource: "parts-catalog" — returns the current list of BioBrick parts
Resource: "codon-tables" — returns codon usage tables for supported organisms
Tool: "run-optimization" — accepts a CDS and returns an optimized sequence
Tool: "order-dna" — submits a DNA sequence to a synthesis vendor

A student claims that Resources and Tools are interchangeable and that
"parts-catalog" could just as easily be a Tool. What is wrong with this claim?

choices.A: Resources are read-only retrieval, while Tools are actions with side effects, so safety review differs
choices.B: Resources must return JSON, while Tools must return text, so they cannot represent the same thing
choices.C: Tools are always faster than Resources, so computational endpoints should be modeled only as Tools
choices.D: Resources are internal-only server objects, so external clients cannot request them through MCP

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: MCP separates Resources (read-only retrieval) from Tools (invoked actions that may have side effects). Read access to a parts catalog should not require the same approval flow as actions like placing an order. Treating read-only data as a Tool creates unnecessary approval friction and blurs an important safety boundary. Option B invents a format rule. Option C invents a performance rule. Option D is false because Resources are intended for client access.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Understanding why proposed actions require explicit approval before execution
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
An MCP-connected assistant is helping a researcher design a plasmid.
The assistant has access to these tools:

Tool: "blast-sequence" — runs BLAST against NCBI (network call)
Tool: "order-dna" — submits a sequence to IDT for synthesis ($$$)
Tool: "write-genbank" — writes a GenBank file to disk

The LLM generates a plan: "I'll BLAST your insert, write the GenBank file,
and order the DNA." Under MCP's controlled invocation model, what should
happen next?

choices.A: All three tools execute immediately because the user already asked for plasmid design help
choices.B: The host presents the proposed tool calls for approval before any of them execute
choices.C: Only order-dna needs approval, while BLAST and file writing run automatically by default
choices.D: The server classifies tool safety and executes whichever calls it considers low risk first

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: In MCP, tool calls are proposed first and executed only after host approval (by the user or by an explicit policy). That approval step matters because the tools here have real side effects: network access, filesystem writes, and a purchase/order action. Option A skips the control boundary. Option C may be possible under a custom policy, but it is not the default controlled-invocation model. Option D incorrectly places the approval decision at the server rather than the host.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Choosing between raising an exception and returning a sentinel value to communicate failure
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
A function validates a DNA sequence before processing:

<python>
# Approach 1: raise on error
def validate_cds(seq):
    if len(seq) % 3 != 0:
        raise ValueError(f'CDS length {len(seq)} is not a multiple of 3')
    return seq

# Approach 2: return None on error
def validate_cds(seq):
    if len(seq) % 3 != 0:
        return None
    return seq
</python>

A caller passes an invalid sequence and then immediately calls
<python>translate()</python> on the result without checking:

<python>
cds = validate_cds('ATGAA')
protein = translate(cds)
</python>

Which approach leads to a more diagnosable failure?

choices.A: Approach 2 is better because returning None gives the caller full control over error handling
choices.B: Approach 1 is better because ValueError stops immediately and explains the detected problem
choices.C: Both approaches are equivalent because translate will raise some error in either case anyway
choices.D: Approach 2 is better because translate automatically skips None and continues processing

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: Approach 1 fails immediately at the point where the invalid input is detected and raises a ValueError that explains the actual problem. Approach 2 silently returns <python>None</python>; if the caller forgets to check, the eventual error appears later inside <python>translate()</python>, often as a less useful TypeError or AttributeError. Option A is about flexibility for careful callers, but the question asks about diagnosability when the caller does not check. Option C ignores the difference in error quality. Option D invents behavior.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Documenting what a function does and does not handle to set correct expectations
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
A function's docstring states:

<python>
def remove_restriction_sites(cds: str, sites: list[str]) -> str:
    '''Silently replaces codons to eliminate the specified restriction
    sites from cds while preserving the encoded protein.

    Scope: operates only on the coding region. Does not modify UTRs,
    promoters, or flanking sequences. Does not check whether the
    replacement introduces new forbidden sites not in the input list.

    Raises ValueError if cds is not a valid reading frame.
    Returns the modified CDS as a string.'''
    ...
</python>

A researcher passes a full plasmid sequence (promoter + CDS + terminator)
and finds that a BsaI site in the promoter is still present after the call.
What went wrong?

choices.A: The function is buggy because it should scan and edit every restriction site in the full input
choices.B: The function was used outside scope because the contract only covers a coding-region sequence
choices.C: The task is impossible because promoter restriction sites cannot be removed by computation
choices.D: The function silently failed because promoter sequence breaks the reading-frame parser logic

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: The docstring explicitly limits the function's scope to the coding region and states that promoters and flanking sequences are not modified. The researcher passed a full plasmid, so the function was used outside its documented contract. This is a usage error, not evidence of a bug in the stated behavior. Option A ignores the documented scope. Option C is factually wrong. Option D invents a failure mode not supported by the contract.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Understanding why storing identifiers rather than full objects enables reliable serialization
api_contracts_and_mcp.question:
A cloning plan is stored as a Python dict and serialized to JSON:

<python>
# Approach 1: embed full objects
plan = {
    'step': 'Gibson',
    'fragments': [polynucleotide_obj_1, polynucleotide_obj_2]
}

# Approach 2: reference by identifier
plan = {
    'step': 'Gibson',
    'fragment_ids': ['frag_001', 'frag_002']
}
</python>

When <python>json.dumps(plan)</python> is called on each approach, what happens?

choices.A: Both plans serialize successfully because json.dumps converts arbitrary Python objects automatically
choices.B: Approach 1 raises TypeError, while approach 2 succeeds because it stores only JSON strings
choices.C: Approach 2 fails during serialization because identifiers must be resolved to objects beforehand
choices.D: Both plans fail because Python dict values cannot be converted into JSON representations

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: <python>json.dumps()</python> can serialize standard JSON-compatible Python types such as dicts, lists, strings, numbers, booleans, and None. Custom objects (like a Polynucleotide instance) are not JSON-serializable by default and raise TypeError unless a custom encoder is provided. Storing identifiers as strings avoids that problem and cleanly separates serialization from later object resolution. Option C confuses serialization with lookup. Option D is false because dicts of JSON-compatible values serialize normally.

api_contracts_and_mcp.topic: Using a schema as a machine-readable contract that enforces structure before data enters a system

choices.A: {"sequence": "ATGCCC", "organism": "ecoli"}
choices.B: {"sequence": "ATGCCC", "organism": "zebrafish"}
choices.C: {"sequence": "ATGCCC"}
choices.D: {"sequence": "ATGCCC", "organism": "ecoli", "temperature": 37}

api_contracts_and_mcp.explanation: Option A includes both required fields, uses a sequence that matches the DNA pattern, and provides an allowed organism value. <python>circular</python> is optional, so omitting it is valid. Option B fails the enum constraint. Option C fails the required-field constraint. Option D fails because <python>additionalProperties: false</python> disallows undeclared keys such as <python>temperature</python>. The schema acts as a machine-readable boundary contract by enforcing structure and allowed values before computation begins.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Identifying which gene layout supports translation of a specified ORF from element order
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
In a bacterial expression construct, which layout is most likely to produce
Protein A? Elements orfA and orfB are ORFs encoding Protein A and Protein B.

choices.A: promoter.rbs.orfA.orfB.terminator
choices.B: promoter.orfA.orfB.terminator
choices.C: promoter.orfA.terminator
choices.D: promoter.rbs.orfB.orfA.terminator

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation:
To produce Protein A, the construct needs transcription (promoter) and an RBS positioned upstream of ORF A so ribosomes can initiate translation at A.

- A has promoter and rbs before A, so A can be transcribed and translated. - B and C lack an rbs before A and thus it won't be translated - D places the rbs before B, so translation is directed to B, not A.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Understanding how enzyme movement on the template determines product directionality
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
RNA polymerase binds a promoter and begins transcribing a gene. The
template strand is read 3'->5', and the new RNA is synthesized 5'->3'.

A student claims the mRNA sequence is identical to the template strand
because polymerase "copies" the template. What is wrong with this claim?

choices.A: The mRNA matches the template strand sequence, but only in reverse orientation
choices.B: RNA polymerase synthesizes RNA without using any DNA strand as a template
choices.C: The mRNA is complementary to the template strand rather than identical to it
choices.D: The mRNA matches the template strand except that uracil replaces thymine

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: RNA polymerase synthesizes an RNA strand that is complementary to the DNA template. Base pairing is A-U, T-A, C-G, and G-C. Therefore, the mRNA is not identical to the template strand. Instead, it matches the coding (non-template) strand sequence, except RNA uses U where DNA uses T. Option D describes the relationship between mRNA and the coding strand, not the template strand.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Distinguishing which strand carries the sequence that matches the product versus which strand is read
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
A gene has the following DNA sequence around the start codon: 
5'-...ATGAAACCC...-3'  (coding strand)
3'-...TACTTTGGG...-5'  (template strand)
Which strand does RNA polymerase physically read, and what is the mRNA sequence for this region?

choices.A: It reads the coding strand (5'->3'); mRNA is 5'-AUGAAACCC...-3'
choices.B: It reads the template strand (3'->5'); mRNA is 5'-UACUUUGGG...-3'
choices.C: It reads the coding strand (3'->5'); mRNA is 5'-CCCAAAGUA...-3'
choices.D: It reads the template strand (3'->5'); mRNA is 5'-AUGAAACCC...-3'

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: RNA polymerase reads the template strand in the 3'->5' direction and synthesizes RNA 5'->3'. The RNA is complementary to the template and thus has the same sequence as the coding strand except U replaces T, giving 5'-AUGAAACCC...-3'. Option A gives the correct mRNA sequence but names the wrong strand as the one polymerase reads.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Reasoning about why molecular differences between DNA and RNA have functional consequences
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
DNA uses thymine (T) while RNA uses uracil (U). Both bases pair with
adenine. A student asks: "If T and U both pair with A, does it matter which
one is used?"

Which answer best explains why the distinction matters biologically?

choices.A: U pairs with A more strongly than T does, making RNA more stable overall
choices.B: U cannot base-pair in duplex regions, so RNA must remain single-stranded
choices.C: T and U are functionally interchangeable, so the choice has no consequence
choices.D: Cells distinguish U from T to support RNA turnover and DNA repair systems

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: T and U differ by a methyl group. That chemical difference matters because it helps cells treat RNA and DNA differently. RNA is meant to be transient in many contexts (for example mRNA turnover), while DNA is long-term storage. Cells also treat uracil in DNA as damage (often arising from cytosine deamination) and repair it. Option A is incorrect, and option C is false because RNA commonly forms double-stranded regions such as hairpins.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Understanding why a lookup table is structured in one direction and what that implies for the reverse problem
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
The genetic code maps codons to amino acids. A student wants to go in the
reverse direction: given an amino acid, determine which DNA codon to use. They
try:

<python>
codon = genetic_code[amino_acid]
</python>

where genetic_code maps codons to amino acids. This fails. Beyond the coding
error, why is the reverse problem fundamentally harder?

choices.A: The reverse mapping is one-to-many because many amino acids have valid codons
choices.B: Some amino acids have no codons in the standard genetic code at all
choices.C: The code only works in 5'->3' translation and cannot be reversed conceptually
choices.D: Amino acids map to codons using a different reading frame than translation

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: The genetic code is degenerate: many amino acids are encoded by multiple synonymous codons. Translation (codon -> amino acid) is many-to-one, so a codon lookup gives a single amino acid. Reverse translation (amino acid -> codon) is one-to-many, so there is not one uniquely correct answer. Choosing among synonymous codons is exactly the codon optimization problem.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Distinguishing the roles of signals that initiate versus terminate a molecular process
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
A gene's CDS begins with ATG and ends with a stop codon (TAA, TAG, or TGA).
A student designs a CDS but accidentally introduces an in-frame TAA codon in
the middle. The gene is transcribed normally. What happens during
translation?

choices.A: The ribosome skips the internal TAA because only terminal stops are recognized
choices.B: The internal TAA encodes an amino acid because codon meaning depends on position
choices.C: Transcription stops at the internal TAA so downstream mRNA is not produced
choices.D: The ribosome stops at the internal TAA and makes a truncated protein product

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: During translation, the ribosome reads codons sequentially from the start codon. Any in-frame stop codon is interpreted as a stop signal. An internal stop codon therefore causes premature termination and yields a truncated protein. Option D confuses translation termination with transcription termination, which is controlled by transcription terminator sequences, not stop codons.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Understanding how the spatial arrangement of gene elements determines functional sequence of events
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
In E. coli, a gene is organized as: 
promoter (-35, -10) -> +1 -> 5'UTR (with RBS) -> ATG -> CDS -> stop -> terminator
A student moves the RBS to a position after the start codon. The promoter, CDS, and terminator are otherwise correct. What is the most likely outcome?

choices.A: Transcription fails because the RBS is required for RNA polymerase binding
choices.B: Translation still starts normally because ribosomes initiate at any nearby ATG
choices.C: mRNA is transcribed, but initiation at the intended ATG is disrupted downstream
choices.D: The protein is produced normally, but the coding region shifts reading frame

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: In bacteria, the ribosome-binding site (Shine-Dalgarno sequence) must be upstream of the start codon so the ribosome can position the start codon correctly for initiation. Moving the RBS downstream disrupts initiation at the intended ATG. Transcription can still occur because RNA polymerase binds the promoter, not the RBS.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Comparing two processes that use template-directed synthesis but produce fundamentally different outputs
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
Replication and transcription both use DNA as a template for polymer
synthesis. A student says: "They are basically the same process, just with a
different polymerase."

Which statement best captures the key difference in what each process
produces?

choices.A: Replication is error-free synthesis; transcription always creates permanent mutations
choices.B: Replication makes RNA intermediates first; transcription makes DNA intermediates first
choices.C: Replication copies coding strands only; transcription copies template strands only
choices.D: Replication makes double-stranded DNA copies; transcription makes single-stranded RNA

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: Replication copies the entire genome to produce new double-stranded DNA molecules. Transcription copies specific regions (genes or operons) into single-stranded RNA. The products differ in chemistry (DNA vs RNA), scope (whole genome vs selected regions), and typical lifespan (genomic storage vs often transient RNA molecules).

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Reasoning about what each type of biological polymer encodes and why the system uses three rather than one
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
DNA, RNA, and protein all carry biological information, but they serve
different roles. A student asks: "Why not just use DNA for everything? It is
stable and stores sequence information."

What is the strongest answer?

choices.A: RNA and protein exist mainly because DNA is too costly to synthesize abundantly
choices.B: Proteins are folded RNAs, so cells effectively rely on only two polymers
choices.C: DNA stores stably, proteins perform functions, and RNA provides regulatable intermediates
choices.D: Evolution has not optimized biology yet, so the three-polymer system persists

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: DNA is specialized for stable information storage. Proteins, built from 20 amino acids with diverse chemistries, perform most catalysis and structural work. RNA serves as an intermediate whose abundance can be regulated without changing DNA copy number, enabling dynamic control of gene expression. The system is not redundant: each polymer contributes a distinct functional role.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Understanding why autonomous genetic elements require both replication and selection functions
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
A minimal plasmid requires at least an origin of replication and a selectable
marker (for example, an antibiotic resistance gene). A student removes the
origin to make room for a larger insert, reasoning that the host chromosome's
origin will replicate the plasmid too.

What is the most likely outcome after transformation?

choices.A: The plasmid is maintained because the host origin replicates all cellular DNA
choices.B: The plasmid is not stably replicated and is lost as transformed cells divide
choices.C: The host cell dies immediately because origin-free plasmids are intrinsically toxic
choices.D: The plasmid integrates at the chromosomal origin without requiring homology

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: A plasmid needs its own origin to be replicated as an independent DNA molecule. The chromosomal origin controls chromosome replication, not random extra DNA molecules. Without a plasmid origin, the incoming plasmid may exist transiently after transformation but is not copied during cell division, so it is progressively lost. Selection cannot maintain a plasmid that is not being replicated.

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.topic: Connecting gene organization to mRNA structure and understanding what one transcript can encode
dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.question:
In E. coli, the lac operon contains three genes (lacZ, lacY, lacA) under one
promoter. When the operon is induced, RNA polymerase transcribes all three
genes into a single mRNA molecule.

How many separate proteins can ribosomes produce from this one mRNA?

choices.A: Three, because each coding region can initiate translation from its own signals
choices.B: One, because a single bacterial mRNA molecule can encode only one protein
choices.C: Three, but only after cleavage into separate RNAs before translation begins
choices.D: It depends entirely on whether all genes share the same reading frame

dna_rna_protein_and_core_processes.explanation: A bacterial polycistronic mRNA can encode multiple proteins. Each coding region can have its own ribosome-binding site and start codon, allowing ribosomes to initiate translation independently on the same transcript. Bacterial translation does not require the mRNA to be cut into separate RNAs first. This arrangement supports coordinated expression of functionally related genes.

regulation_control_points.topic: Identifying the direct molecular role of the bacterial ribosome binding site in gene expression
regulation_control_points.question:
Which statement most directly describes the role of a bacterial ribosome binding
site (RBS)?

choices.A: Site where transcription begins on DNA
choices.B: Sequence that ends transcription on DNA
choices.C: mRNA site that helps start translation
choices.D: DNA origin that starts plasmid replication
choices.E: Sequence that sets plasmid copy number

regulation_control_points.explanation:
A bacterial ribosome binding site (RBS) is an mRNA sequence that promotes translation initiation by helping the small ribosomal subunit bind near the start codon. In bacteria, this commonly involves base-pairing between the Shine-Dalgarno sequence in the mRNA and the anti-Shine-Dalgarno sequence in 16S rRNA.

Promoters control transcription initiation, terminators control transcription termination, and origins of replication control DNA replication rather than gene expression directly.

regulation_control_points.topic: Identifying which molecular features of a promoter determine transcription initiation rate
regulation_control_points.question:
You are comparing two promoters that drive expression of the same gene in E. coli. Promoter A has a -10 box of 
TATAAT
and a -35 box of 
TTGACA
. Promoter B has a -10 box of 
CATAAT
and a -35 box of 
TTGACA
. Both use sigma-70. Promoter A produces 10x more transcript than Promoter B. Which explanation best accounts for the difference?

choices.A: The -10 box change alters the mRNA leader, destabilizing the ribosome binding site
choices.B: Sigma-70 recognizes only the -35 box, so the -10 mutation should not matter
choices.C: The -10 mutation changes coding sequence identity, altering the downstream protein product
choices.D: The -10 base change weakens sigma-70 binding, reducing RNA polymerase recruitment and initiation

regulation_control_points.explanation:
Sigma factor recognizes both the -10 and -35 promoter elements. 
TATAAT
is the sigma-70 consensus -10 sequence, so changing it to 
CATAAT
weakens promoter recognition and reduces formation of a productive open complex. That lowers transcription initiation rate. This is a transcriptional control point. Option B confuses promoter DNA with the transcribed mRNA region that contains the RBS. Option C is incorrect because sigma factor contacts both boxes. Option D is incorrect because promoter sequence is upstream of the coding region and is not translated.

regulation_control_points.topic: Reasoning about how Shine-Dalgarno sequence complementarity affects translation rate
regulation_control_points.question:
Two constructs encode the same protein but differ only in their ribosome binding site (RBS) region. Construct 1 has the Shine-Dalgarno sequence 
AGGAGG
at position -8 relative to the start codon. Construct 2 has 
ACCACC
at the same position. Both produce identical mRNA levels. What is the most likely effect on protein expression?

choices.A: Construct 2 makes more protein because ACCACC prevents ribosome pausing during initiation
choices.B: Both constructs make equal protein because their measured mRNA levels are identical
choices.C: Construct 1 makes more protein because AGGAGG pairs better with 16S rRNA
choices.D: Construct 2 makes more protein because its RBS recruits extra ribosomes simultaneously

regulation_control_points.explanation:
The Shine-Dalgarno sequence promotes translation initiation by base-pairing with the anti-Shine-Dalgarno sequence at the 3' end of 16S rRNA. 
AGGAGG
is a strong consensus-like SD sequence and provides stronger complementarity than 
ACCACC
, which reduces ribosome recruitment and initiation. Equal mRNA levels do not imply equal protein output, because translation initiation is an independent control point. Option A and D do not provide a valid mechanism. Option B incorrectly treats transcription and translation as the same process.

regulation_control_points.topic: Understanding how mRNA secondary structure near the start codon can occlude the RBS
regulation_control_points.question:
You design a construct with a strong Shine-Dalgarno sequence and optimal spacing
to the start codon. However, an RNA folding prediction shows that the SD sequence
is sequestered in a stable hairpin formed between the SD region and nucleotides
downstream of the start codon. Protein expression is very low despite high mRNA
levels.

What is the most likely cause?

choices.A: The hairpin functions as a terminator, causing RNA polymerase to stop upstream
choices.B: The hairpin hides the SD sequence, blocking 30S ribosome binding and initiation
choices.C: The hairpin changes codon identity in the coding sequence during transcription
choices.D: The hairpin guarantees RNase E cleavage before any translation can begin

regulation_control_points.explanation:
Translation initiation requires the 30S ribosomal subunit to access the Shine-Dalgarno sequence and base-pair with 16S rRNA. If the SD sequence is trapped in a stable hairpin, the ribosome cannot bind efficiently, so translation initiation is strongly reduced even if the SD sequence itself is strong.

This is why secondary structure near the start region (often approximated as the window around the start codon) is a major determinant of expression. Option A describes a terminator mechanism, which requires the right sequence context and location. Option C confuses sequence with structure. Option D is possible in some cases, but it does not best explain low protein together with high mRNA.

regulation_control_points.topic: Reasoning about how synthesis and degradation rates jointly determine steady-state mRNA concentration
regulation_control_points.question:
Two genes are transcribed at the same rate from identical promoters. Gene A's mRNA
has a half-life of 2 minutes, while Gene B's mRNA has a half-life of 20 minutes.
Both are measured at steady state.

What is the expected relationship between their mRNA levels?

choices.A: Gene A has ~10x more mRNA because rapid turnover drives faster replacement
choices.B: Gene B has ~10x more mRNA because it is degraded much more slowly
choices.C: Both genes have equal mRNA because their promoter-driven transcription rates match
choices.D: Gene A has more mRNA because short transcripts are preferentially ribosome-protected

regulation_control_points.explanation:
At steady state, mRNA abundance depends on both synthesis and degradation. If transcription rates are equal, the mRNA with the longer half-life accumulates to a higher steady-state level because it is removed more slowly.

Since Gene B's mRNA half-life is 10x longer (20 min vs 2 min), Gene B is expected to have roughly 10x more mRNA at steady state. Option A reverses the relationship. Option C ignores degradation. Option D is not a general rule and does not explain the stated comparison.

regulation_control_points.topic: Understanding how synonymous codon choice affects protein expression independently of regulation
regulation_control_points.question:
Two constructs encode the exact same GFP protein with identical promoters, RBS
sequences, and terminators. The only difference is that Construct 1 uses codons
abundant in E. coli, while Construct 2 uses rare codons throughout the CDS.
Construct 1 produces 100x more fluorescence.

Which statement best explains this result?

choices.A: Rare codons block transcription because RNA polymerase cannot pass coding regions
choices.B: Rare codons alter the Shine-Dalgarno sequence, preventing translation initiation entirely
choices.C: Rare codons change amino acid identity, producing a different and inactive GFP
choices.D: Rare codons slow elongation and can reduce completed protein production and mRNA stability

regulation_control_points.explanation:
Synonymous codons encode the same amino acid sequence, so the protein identity is unchanged. However, codon choice can strongly affect expression by changing translation elongation kinetics. Rare codons are decoded by lower-abundance tRNAs, which can slow ribosomes and reduce the rate of completed protein production. In some contexts, this can also contribute to reduced mRNA stability.

Option A is incorrect because synonymous codons do not change the amino acid sequence. Option C is incorrect because RNA polymerase does not read codons during transcription. Option D is incorrect because the Shine-Dalgarno sequence is in the 5' UTR, not in the coding sequence.

regulation_control_points.topic: Distinguishing which molecular properties change vs. remain constant when an RBS part is swapped
regulation_control_points.question:
You are tuning expression of an enzyme in a metabolic pathway. You swap the RBS in
your construct from a strong RBS (high translation initiation rate) to a weak RBS
(low translation initiation rate). The CDS, promoter, and terminator remain
unchanged.

Which of the following changes as a result of this swap?

choices.A: The enzyme amino acid sequence, because translation starts with different efficiency
choices.B: Pathway metabolite concentrations, because enzyme abundance changes after RBS replacement
choices.C: The enzyme's post-translational modification pattern, because the mRNA leader changed
choices.D: The enzyme's catalytic rate constant, because ribosomes translate more slowly

regulation_control_points.explanation:
Swapping the RBS changes translation initiation rate, which changes how much enzyme is produced. Changing enzyme abundance can change pathway flux and therefore alter metabolite concentrations.

The RBS is in the 5' UTR, so it does not change the coding sequence and therefore does not change the amino acid sequence (A). It also does not change the intrinsic catalytic activity per molecule (D). Option C is not the expected consequence of an RBS swap in this context.

regulation_control_points.topic: Classifying genetic parts by whether they act at the transcriptional or translational level
regulation_control_points.question:
A student is debugging a construct that produces no detectable protein. They
measure mRNA and find abundant transcript. They then inspect the 5' UTR and find
that the Shine-Dalgarno sequence has been deleted.

At which level of gene expression is the problem?

choices.A: Translational, because deleting the SD sequence blocks ribosome initiation on mRNA
choices.B: Transcriptional, because the promoter can no longer recruit RNA polymerase efficiently
choices.C: Post-translational, because the protein is degraded immediately after synthesis occurs
choices.D: Transcriptional, because SD deletion prevents proper RNA polymerase termination downstream

regulation_control_points.explanation:
Abundant mRNA indicates that transcription is occurring, so the promoter is working. The Shine-Dalgarno sequence is needed for efficient ribosome recruitment to the mRNA. If the SD sequence is deleted, translation initiation fails and little or no protein is produced despite abundant transcript.

Option A is contradicted by the mRNA measurement. Option C would require protein to be synthesized first. Option D confuses translation initiation signals with transcription termination.

regulation_control_points.topic: Explaining why identical proteins can have different expression levels from different DNA encodings
regulation_control_points.question:
A lab orders two synthetic genes encoding the identical lysozyme protein. Gene
version 1 is codon-optimized for E. coli. Gene version 2 uses the original
bacteriophage codon usage. Both are cloned into the same vector with the same
promoter, RBS, and terminator. Version 1 produces 50x more lysozyme.

What is the BEST explanation for why two DNA sequences encoding the same protein
yield such different expression levels?

choices.A: Different DNA codons produce different amino acid sequences for the same protein
choices.B: Codon-optimized genes include stronger promoters that increase transcription initiation rates
choices.C: The phage-derived coding sequence contains a weaker Shine-Dalgarno motif internally
choices.D: Different codons alter mRNA structure and elongation kinetics without changing protein identity

regulation_control_points.explanation:
Synonymous codon changes alter the mRNA sequence without changing the amino acid sequence. That can change local mRNA secondary structure near the start codon (which affects translation initiation) and can also change elongation rate because codons differ in tRNA availability.

These effects can produce large expression differences even when promoter, RBS, and terminator are unchanged. Option A is incorrect because the proteins are stated to be identical. Option C is incorrect because both constructs use the same promoter. Option D is incorrect because the RBS is in the 5' UTR and is the same in both constructs.

regulation_control_points.topic: Understanding how terminators define the 3' boundary of a transcript and prevent read-through
regulation_control_points.question:
An operon contains Gene A followed by Gene B, each with its own RBS and start
codon. A strong Rho-independent terminator is placed between Gene A and Gene B.
When the terminator is present, Gene B protein levels are very low. When the
terminator is removed, Gene B is expressed at high levels from the same promoter
driving Gene A.

What role is the terminator playing?

choices.A: The terminator targets Gene B protein for degradation after translation is complete
choices.B: The terminator hairpin blocks ribosomes from reaching Gene B start codon directly
choices.C: The terminator stops transcription before Gene B, so Gene B lacks mRNA
choices.D: The terminator shifts the reading frame of Gene B during translation initiation

regulation_control_points.explanation:
A Rho-independent terminator forms a stable RNA hairpin followed by a U-rich tract, which promotes dissociation of RNA polymerase from the DNA template. When the terminator is placed between Gene A and Gene B, it stops transcription before Gene B is transcribed, so Gene B lacks mRNA and is not expressed.

Removing the terminator allows transcriptional read-through into Gene B, producing a polycistronic transcript from which Gene B can be translated using its own RBS. Option A confuses transcription with protein degradation. Option B confuses termination with RBS occlusion. Option D has no mechanistic basis.

regulation_control_points.topic: Reasoning about how RNA degradation rate contributes to steady-state protein expression
regulation_control_points.question:
You are engineering a biosensor whose output protein should drop quickly after an
inducer signal disappears. You want the protein level to decay rapidly once
transcription stops.

Which design strategy would MOST help achieve fast signal decay?

choices.A: Use the strongest RBS possible to maximize translation before transcript decay
choices.B: Remove the terminator so read-through transcription increases total mRNA production
choices.C: Use a highly stable mRNA so translation continues efficiently after induction ends
choices.D: Add RNase-sensitive mRNA features and a protein degradation tag for rapid turnover

regulation_control_points.explanation:
Fast signal decay requires rapid clearance of both the mRNA and the protein after transcription stops. If the mRNA remains stable, translation can continue after the inducer is gone, which slows the response. Adding RNase E-sensitive features can shorten mRNA lifetime, and adding a protein degradation tag increases protein turnover.

Option C can increase expression output but does not improve decay kinetics. Option D increases transcript production, which opposes the design goal. Temporal control of expression depends on both synthesis and degradation processes.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Distinguishing between a strain name, the living cell population it refers to, and the genotype that describes it
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
A lab maintains a freezer stock labeled "Mach1." A new student asks whether
"Mach1" refers to the specific mutations (recA1398, endA1, etc.) or to the
population of cells in the tube.

Which answer is most precise?

choices.A: Mach1 is the genotype, because the name itself stands for the full list of mutations present in the cells
choices.B: Mach1 is a phenotype, because it names what the cells do in culture rather than the DNA changes they carry
choices.C: Mach1 is a strain, meaning a genetically homogeneous cell population; the genotype is the mutation list relative to a parent
choices.D: Mach1 is a composition, because it specifies which biological parts should exist without describing a living population

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
A strain is a population-level concept: a genetically uniform group of cells traced to a single colony (or clone). The genotype is the set of genetic differences, usually described relative to a parent strain, that characterizes that strain.

Option A conflates the strain name with the genotype description. Option C confuses genotype with phenotype. Option D confuses a living biological strain with an abstract design specification.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Identifying what an abstract biological specification commits to and what it leaves unresolved
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
A Composition for a biosynthetic pathway specifies:
- Host: E. coli
- Proteins: LuxA, LuxB, LuxC, LuxD, LuxE

A student assumes this Composition also determines the promoter driving each
gene and whether the genes are on a plasmid or integrated into the chromosome.
Is the student correct?

choices.A: Yes, because a useful Composition must include every implementation detail, including promoters, vectors, and genomic placement
choices.B: No, because a Composition specifies host and target proteins but leaves promoter choice, regulation, codon usage, and locus open
choices.C: No, because a Composition specifies only the exact DNA sequence and does not directly specify proteins or host context
choices.D: Yes, because once the host organism is chosen, promoter identity and genomic versus plasmid location are automatically fixed

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
A Composition is an abstract specification. It commits to what should be present (for example, host and protein identities) but intentionally leaves open many implementation decisions such as promoters, RBSs, copy number, codon usage, and whether the build is plasmid-borne or chromosomally integrated.

This separation is useful because the same Composition can be implemented in multiple ways. Option A confuses an abstract specification with a complete build plan. Option C reverses the abstraction level. Option D incorrectly assumes host choice determines all downstream design choices.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Understanding why genotype descriptions emphasize changes relative to a parent strain
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
The genotype of Mach1 is written as: 
str. W deltarecA1398 endA1 fhuA phi80delta(lac)M15 delta(lac)X74 hsdR(rK- mK+)
A student argues it would be simpler to store the complete genome sequence instead of these cryptic delta annotations. What is the main reason genotypes are often expressed as changes from a parent strain?

choices.A: Relative notation is necessary because the complete E. coli genome sequence is still unknown for common laboratory strains
choices.B: Relative notation highlights behavior-relevant mutations, while full genomes bury a few important differences among many unchanged bases
choices.C: Relative notation is required by regulation, so engineered strains must be documented using delta-based annotations by law
choices.D: Delta notation is mainly a storage shortcut so genotype records fit into small database fields and labels more easily

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
Relative genotype notation is useful because it emphasizes the changes that distinguish the strain from its parent and often explain its engineering properties. A complete genome sequence contains far more information than is usually needed for routine strain identity and engineering context, and the few relevant differences are hard to spot without a comparison.

Option A treats the notation as a storage trick rather than an information and interpretation tool. Option C is false. Option D is fabricated.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Reasoning about why antibiotic selection depends on compatibility between host background and vector marker
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
A researcher wants to transform a plasmid carrying a kanamycin resistance gene
(KanR) into a strain that already has a chromosomal KanR insertion from a
previous engineering step. They plan to select transformants on kanamycin
plates.

What is wrong with this plan?

choices.A: Chromosomal KanR does not function for antibiotic resistance, so only plasmid-encoded KanR can support colony growth
choices.B: Two KanR copies are usually toxic to cells, so transformants will die before they can form colonies on selection plates
choices.C: Nothing is wrong, because extra KanR copies should make transformed cells grow much faster than untransformed host cells
choices.D: The host is already kanamycin resistant, so transformed and untransformed cells both survive and selection cannot distinguish them

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
Antibiotic selection works only if cells lacking the plasmid are sensitive to the antibiotic. If the host strain already carries a functional chromosomal KanR gene, untransformed cells also survive on kanamycin, so the plate no longer selects for plasmid uptake.

The practical fix is to use a different selectable marker that the host does not already carry. Option A misunderstands the purpose of selection. Option C is not generally true. Option D is factually incorrect.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Understanding how one abstract specification can be implemented by multiple distinct genotypes
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
Two labs independently implement the same Composition:
- Host: E. coli
- Proteins: GFP, LacI

Lab A puts both genes on a high-copy plasmid with strong promoters.
Lab B integrates both genes into the chromosome under weak promoters with
codon optimization for E. coli.

Both labs produce cells that express GFP and LacI. How do their results relate
to the Composition?

choices.A: Only Lab A satisfies the Composition, because plasmid expression is the standard implementation and therefore the intended one
choices.B: Both satisfy the Composition, but they produced different genotypes and likely different phenotypes from those genotypes
choices.C: Neither lab satisfies the Composition, because using different promoter and copy-number strategies changes the actual design
choices.D: Both produced the same genotype, because starting from the same Composition determines a single correct DNA implementation

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
A Composition specifies what is required at a high level (here, host and protein identities), not the detailed implementation strategy. Both labs can satisfy the same Composition while making different choices about promoter strength, copy number, codon usage, and genomic location.

Those choices produce different DNA architectures and therefore different genotypes, and they often produce different phenotypes such as expression level, growth rate, and burden. Option A inserts a preference not stated by the Composition. Option B misunderstands what it means to satisfy a spec. Option D confuses abstract specification with concrete genotype.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Reasoning about how chassis choice constrains which regulatory elements and expression mechanisms are valid
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
A researcher designs a gene expression cassette using an E. coli sigma-70
promoter and a strong E. coli ribosome binding site. They then move the same
cassette into Saccharomyces cerevisiae (yeast) without any modifications.

What is most likely to happen?

choices.A: The gene will likely fail to express, because yeast does not use sigma-70 promoters or Shine-Dalgarno translation initiation
choices.B: The gene should express normally, because promoter motifs and translation-initiation signals are broadly universal across cells
choices.C: The gene may express somewhat less due to codon bias, but the bacterial promoter and RBS should still function in yeast
choices.D: The gene will express but the protein will be nonfunctional, because yeast ribosomes translate bacterial genes too quickly

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
Chassis choice constrains what regulatory parts are valid. Yeast and bacteria use different transcription and translation initiation machinery. Yeast RNA polymerase does not recognize bacterial sigma-70 promoters, and yeast translation initiation does not rely on bacterial Shine-Dalgarno pairing.

A yeast-compatible implementation would require yeast regulatory elements, including an appropriate promoter and translation initiation context. Option A assumes universality that does not exist. Option B underestimates the problem. Option D proposes an incorrect mechanism.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Identifying host strain features that improve routine plasmid cloning and DNA handling
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
Mach1 carries several mutations including recA1398 (defective
recombination), endA1 (defective endonuclease), and hsdR(rK- mK+)
(does not restrict incoming DNA but still methylates DNA).

Why are these mutations helpful for plasmid-based genetic engineering,
rather than being random changes?

choices.A: They mainly provide auxotrophic markers so transformed cells can be selected on minimal medium without antibiotics
choices.B: They mainly improve heat-shock survival, allowing cells to tolerate higher temperatures during transformation steps
choices.C: They reduce host activities that would otherwise rearrange, degrade, or restrict incoming plasmid and foreign DNA
choices.D: They mainly increase growth speed, which is the primary design goal for strains used in routine cloning workflows

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
Cloning strains are engineered to make foreign DNA easier to introduce, maintain, and recover. In this example, recA defects reduce homologous recombination that can rearrange repeated sequences, endA1 improves plasmid DNA quality by reducing nuclease activity, and hsdR defects reduce restriction of incoming DNA.

These are deliberate engineering traits for molecular cloning workflows. Option A is not the main purpose. Option C confuses cloning-strain features with nutritional markers. Option D confuses genotype with protocol details.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Interpreting genotype notation tokens and connecting notation to physical DNA changes
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
A genotype includes the token "delta(lac)X74." A student interprets this as
meaning the lac region is overexpressed because "delta" sounds like it could
mean "enhanced."

What does the delta symbol indicate in genotype notation, and what is the
physical consequence?

choices.A: Delta means translocation, so the lac region was moved intact to a different chromosome position in the strain
choices.B: Delta means promoter replacement, so lac genes remain present but are controlled by a different regulatory sequence
choices.C: Delta means duplication, so the lac region is present in an extra chromosomal copy in this strain background
choices.D: Delta means deletion, so the designated lac region has been physically removed from the chromosome sequence

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
In genotype notation, delta denotes a deletion. The named region has been removed from the chromosome, so the strain lacks that DNA segment and cannot produce whatever gene products depend on the deleted sequence.

Option A invents a meaning for delta. Option C describes a translocation, not a deletion. Option D describes a regulatory edit that would be represented with different notation.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Reasoning about how the same cassette can produce different outcomes in different genomic contexts
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
A researcher characterizes a promoter-RBS-GFP cassette on a high-copy plasmid
(about 100 copies per cell) in E. coli strain DH10B and measures strong
fluorescence. They then integrate the identical cassette as a single
chromosomal copy in the same strain. Fluorescence drops by about 100-fold.

What is the best explanation?

choices.A: Copy number changed from about 100 templates to one, so total mRNA and protein output per cell drops substantially
choices.B: Integration usually damages the GFP coding region, so fluorescence drops because the encoded protein is no longer functional
choices.C: Chromosomal DNA is not efficiently transcribed, so RNA polymerase cannot produce enough mRNA from integrated genes
choices.D: The promoter functions only on circular plasmids, so it becomes inactive when the same cassette is chromosomal

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
The cassette sequence is the same, but the genomic context changed. Copy number is a major contextual variable. A high-copy plasmid provides many templates for transcription, while a single chromosomal insertion provides only one. With far fewer transcription templates, total mRNA and protein output usually drops.

This is a core example of why identical parts can produce different phenotypes in different contexts. Option A is not the expected explanation. Option C is false. Option D is false.

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.topic: Identifying what semantic design information is missing from a raw DNA sequence string
genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.question:
A collaborator sends you a 3,000 bp DNA string with no annotation. You know it
encodes a gene, but you do not know what organism it was designed for or how it
should be used. Compared to receiving a Composition object that specifies
"Host: E. coli, Protein: LuxA", what information are you missing from the raw
DNA string alone?

choices.A: You are missing only the host, because the intended protein can always be inferred unambiguously from sequence translation
choices.B: Nothing important is missing, because translating the DNA sequence recovers all design meaning needed for implementation
choices.C: You are missing high-level intent and context, including intended host, target protein identity, and implementation assumptions
choices.D: Nothing important is missing, because raw DNA always contains more useful information than an abstract Composition object

genome_strain_and_chassis_constraints.explanation:
A raw DNA string contains sequence-level information, but it does not encode the intended design semantics in a directly interpretable way. Without annotation or additional context, you may not know the intended host, the intended protein identity, the correct coding frame and start site, or how the sequence fits into a larger design plan.

A Composition explicitly provides high-level design intent such as host and target protein identity. Option A and C overstate what can be inferred from a bare sequence alone. Option D misses the distinction between low-level sequence detail and high-level design meaning.

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.topic: Applying a sequence transformation to make hidden information visible
targeting_rules_and_design_logic.question:
What is the reverse complement of the DNA sequence: 
ATCGAGA

choices.A: 3'-ATCGAGA-5'
choices.B: 5'-ATCGAGA-3'
choices.C: 5'-TAGCTCT-3'
choices.D: 5'-TCTCGAT-3'

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.explanation:
The reverse complement is found in two steps. First, take the complement of each base (A↔T, C↔G), so 
ATCGAGA
becomes 
TAGCTCT
. Then reverse that string so the opposite strand is written in the standard 5'→3' orientation, giving 
TCTCGAT
. Option D is the complement only (not reversed). Option A is the original sequence with flipped end labels, not the reverse complement. Option C is the unchanged input.

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.topic: Understanding how a recognition rule limits where a tool can act on a sequence
targeting_rules_and_design_logic.question:
A Cas9 CRISPR system requires an NGG PAM adjacent to the target site.
A researcher wants to knock out a gene but finds no NGG motif within the
first 200 bp of the coding sequence on either strand.

What is the most accurate conclusion?

choices.A: The researcher should first engineer an NGG motif into the gene and then use standard Cas9 targeting there
choices.B: Cas9 can still target this region if the guide is extended beyond 20 nt to improve binding specificity
choices.C: Cas9 cannot target this region, so they must choose a different CRISPR system or a site farther downstream
choices.D: The PAM rule only needs to be checked on the forward strand, because guides determine strand selection later

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.explanation:
The PAM is a fixed requirement of the Cas9 protein and must be present in the genomic DNA adjacent to the target. Without an NGG on either strand, Cas9 has no valid binding site in that region.

Option B is not a practical solution to the targeting problem as stated. Option C is wrong because guide length does not override the PAM requirement. Option D is wrong because DNA is double-stranded and a valid PAM can be on either strand.

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.topic: Translating a biological positional rule into correct sequence indices
targeting_rules_and_design_logic.question:
A function searches a target DNA string for the Cas9 PAM and extracts
the 20 bp protospacer. Given:

<python>
target = 'ATGCCCTAGATGCCTGGCTCAGAAACCTGCC'
pam_index = target.find('GG', 21)   # returns 24
</python>

Which slice correctly extracts the protospacer?

choices.A: target[pam_index - 20 : pam_index]
choices.B: target[pam_index - 21 : pam_index - 1]
choices.C: target[pam_index - 20 : pam_index - 1]
choices.D: target[pam_index - 21 : pam_index]

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.explanation:
For Cas9, the PAM is NGG and the N is not part of the protospacer. If <python>pam_index</python> points to the first G of GG, then the N is at <python>pam_index - 1</python>. The 20 bp protospacer ends immediately before that N.

Therefore the protospacer spans indices <python>pam_index - 21</python> through <python>pam_index - 2</python> inclusive, which in Python slicing is <python>target[pam_index - 21 : pam_index - 1]</python>.

Option B includes the PAM-adjacent N. Option C is only 19 bp. Option D is 21 bp and also includes the N.

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.topic: Reasoning about how an indexing error propagates into a biological design failure
targeting_rules_and_design_logic.question:
A student's gRNA design function extracts the protospacer as:

<python>
protospacer = target[pam_index - 20 : pam_index]
</python>

instead of the correct <python>target[pam_index - 21 : pam_index - 1]</python>.
The resulting guide RNA is synthesized and tested. What is the most likely
outcome?

choices.A: The guide is shifted by one base toward the PAM and may overlap it, which often reduces or abolishes editing
choices.B: The guide works the same in practice because a one-base shift is usually tolerated during Cas9 targeting
choices.C: The code fails immediately with an IndexError because the slice end no longer matches the PAM boundary rule
choices.D: The designed guide is only 19 bases long, so synthesis fails before the targeting experiment is performed

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.explanation:
The incorrect slice still returns 20 bases, but it includes the N of the NGG PAM and omits the first base of the true protospacer. That shifts the guide by one base toward the PAM.

Cas9 targeting depends on the correct protospacer sequence adjacent to (not overlapping) the PAM, so this shift will often severely reduce or eliminate editing. Option C is wrong because the slice is syntactically valid. Option D is wrong because the slice length is still 20.

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.topic: Recognizing when a search must consider complementary representations of the same data
targeting_rules_and_design_logic.question:
A function searches for Cas9 PAM sites on a target gene:

<python>
def find_pam(target):
    return target.find('GG', 21)
</python>

It returns -1 for a known targetable gene. A colleague points out the
error. What did the function fail to do?

choices.A: Search for CC on the forward strand so it can detect the complement of GG without computing strand reversal
choices.B: Use a regular expression search instead of .find() so degenerate PAM patterns can be matched automatically
choices.C: Start searching at index 0 rather than 21 so early PAMs are not missed in short coding sequences
choices.D: Search the reverse complement for GG so PAMs on the opposite strand are checked with the correct geometry

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.explanation:
DNA is double-stranded, so a valid PAM can occur on either strand. If the forward strand search finds no valid GG in the required position range, a PAM may still exist on the opposite strand and will be found by searching the reverse complement.

Option B is not equivalent, because simply searching for <python>'CC'</python> on the forward strand does not preserve the correct spatial relationship between PAM and protospacer. Option C does not fix the strand-coverage error. Option D changes the search window but not the missing-strand logic.

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.topic: Tracing a data transformation through multiple encoding steps
targeting_rules_and_design_logic.question:
Given a DNA target with protospacer 
CATCGCCGCAGCGGTTTCAG
adjacent to an NGG PAM, a function must produce the guide RNA. The gRNA scaffold is a constant string appended after the targeting sequence. Which step correctly converts the protospacer to the targeting portion of the gRNA?

choices.A: Reverse-complement the protospacer first, then replace T with U to generate the strand that binds the target
choices.B: Translate the protospacer into amino acids, then encode that peptide as RNA for the targeting region sequence
choices.C: Replace each T with U so the protospacer is converted directly into the RNA version of the same sequence
choices.D: Reverse the protospacer without complementing, then replace T with U before appending the scaffold sequence

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.explanation:
In this convention, the protospacer is the DNA sequence corresponding to the guide-targeting region. To generate the gRNA targeting sequence, convert that DNA sequence to RNA by replacing T with U.

Option B incorrectly reverse-complements the protospacer. Option C confuses guide design with protein translation. Option D reverses the sequence without biological justification.

targeting_rules_and_design_logic.topic: Recognizing how changing a system's rules changes the algorithm that implements it
targeting_rules_and_design_logic.question:
Cas9 uses an NGG PAM on the 3' side of the protospacer, while Cas12a
uses a TTTV PAM on the 5' side. A student has a working Cas9 gRNA
design function. Which change is necessary to adapt it for Cas12a?

choices.A: Replace the literal string GG with TTTV but keep the same extraction indices because only the motif changes
choices.B: Search for a TTTV PAM and extract the protospacer on the downstream side because Cas12a uses 5'-side PAMs
